[v0.3.21](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/releases/tag/v0.3.21)

## What's new

- **Fix: Codex hook JSON schema** — `systemMessage` now placed at the top level of the hook output, not inside `hookSpecificOutput`. codex-cli 0.118.0+ uses `additionalProperties: false` on the inner object so the old placement caused a validation error ( [#138](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/issues/138))
- **Fix: git hooks now use `#!/bin/sh`** instead of `#!/bin/bash` — Git for Windows ships `sh.exe`, not bash, so hooks were silently skipped after every commit on Windows ( [#140](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/issues/140))

## Install / upgrade

```
pip install --upgrade graphifyy
```

Then re-run `graphify codex install` to get the fixed hook, and `graphify hook install` to get the fixed git hook.

Full changelog: [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)

[v0.3.20](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/releases/tag/v0.3.20)

## What's new

- **Fix: XSS in interactive HTML graph** — node labels, file types, community names, source files, and edge relations now HTML-escaped before `innerHTML` injection; neighbor link `onclick` uses `JSON.stringify` instead of raw string interpolation
- **Add: OpenCode `tool.execute.before` plugin** — `graphify opencode install` now writes `.opencode/plugins/graphify.js` and registers it in `opencode.json`, firing the graph reminder before bash calls (equivalent to Claude Code's PreToolUse hook) ( [#71](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/issues/71))
- **Fix: AST call edges now `EXTRACTED/1.0`** instead of INFERRED/0.8 — tree-sitter call resolution is deterministic, not probabilistic ( [#127](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/pull/127))
- **Fix: `tree-sitter>=0.23.0` pinned** and `_check_tree_sitter_version()` guard added — stale environments now get a clear error with upgrade instructions instead of a cryptic `TypeError` ( [#89](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/pull/89))

## Install / upgrade

```
pip install --upgrade graphifyy
```

Full changelog: [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md)

[v0.3.18](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/releases/tag/v0.3.18)

## Fixes

- **Watch + .graphifyignore**: `--watch` mode now respects `.graphifyignore` — `_rebuild_code` was calling `collect_files()` directly instead of `detect()`, bypassing ignore patterns ( [#120](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/issues/120))
- **Codex PreToolUse hook**: now uses `systemMessage` instead of `additionalContext` — Codex does not support `additionalContext` and was returning an error ( [#121](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/issues/121))
- **Trae link**: corrected from `trae.com` to `trae.ai` across all READMEs ( [#122](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/issues/122))
- **HTML graph click detection**: hover-tracking (`hoveredNodeId`) replaces unreliable vis.js click params — small/dense nodes now reliably selectable ( [#82](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/pull/82))
- **`.graphify_python` persistence**: `mkdir -p graphify-out` runs before writing `.graphify_python`; file no longer deleted in Step 9 cleanup — pipx users no longer hit `ModuleNotFoundError` on follow-up commands ( [#93](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/pull/93))
- **Trae skill packaging**: `skill-trae.md` added to `pyproject.toml` package-data — Trae users were getting `ModuleNotFoundError` after `pip install` ( [#102](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/pull/102))
- **Extension drift**: `analyze.py` and `watch.py` now import extension sets from `detect.py` instead of local copies — Swift, Lua, Zig, PowerShell, Elixir, JSX, Julia, Objective-C files were being misclassified as documents ( [#109](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/pull/109))

## Additions

- `graphify save-result` CLI subcommand — saves Q&A results to memory dir; replaces inline Python blocks in all 6 skill files ( [#114](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/issues/114))
- Korean README added (README.ko-KR.md) ( [#112](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/issues/112))
- Interpreter guard section in `skill.md` for when `graphify-out/` is deleted before follow-up commands ( [#93](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/pull/93))

## Refactors

- Dead `build_graph()` function removed from `cluster.py` ( [#109](https://github.com/safishamsi/graphify/pull/109))

## Install

```
pip install graphifyy==0.3.18 && graphify install
```

## 0.3.15

- Feat: Trae and Trae CN platform support (`graphify install --platform trae` / `trae-cn`)
- Fix: `skill-droid.md` was missing from PyPI package data — Factory Droid users couldn't install the skill
- Fix: XSS in HTML legend — community labels now HTML-escaped before `innerHTML` injection

- Fix: graspologic ANSI escape codes no longer corrupt PowerShell 5.1 scroll buffer
- Docs: Japanese README added
- Docs: `graph.json` \+ LLM workflow example added to README

content.text:
This changes the picture significantly. Let me update my assessment with the release data:

---

## Updated Assessment: Graphify Is Moving Fast — Too Fast for a Foundation

**The numbers tell a clear story:**

| Metric | Value | Signal |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Stars** | 16,600 | Massive traction — this project has community momentum |
| **Versions** | v0.1.9 → v0.3.24 in 4 days | ~60 releases in one week |
| **Today alone** | v0.3.15 → v0.3.24 (10 releases) | Hyperspeed iteration |
| **Platform support** | Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Cursor, Gemini CLI, Trae, Factory Droid | Aggressive expansion |

**But the bug pattern reveals instability:**

- **v0.3.22**: `_rebuild_code()` KeyError — git hooks **silently failing on every commit**
- **v0.3.22**: Crash on NetworkX 3.2.x — compatibility shim missing at call sites
- **v0.3.21**: Git hooks used `#!/bin/bash` — **broken on Windows entirely**
- **v0.3.20**: **XSS vulnerability** in HTML graph (innerHTML injection)
- **v0.3.18**: Watch mode **bypassed .graphifyignore** — called wrong function
- **v0.3.18**: Extension drift — Swift, Lua, Zig, Elixir files **misclassified as documents**
- **v0.3.15**: Another **XSS in HTML legend**
- **v0.3.15**: Shebang metacharacter injection vulnerability

This is a project in its "move fast and break things" phase. The traction is real. The engineering talent is real. But the stability is not there yet — security vulnerabilities are still being discovered weekly, core features (watch mode, git hooks) had fundamental bugs, and the API surface is shifting with every release.

---

## Refined Architectural Position

My layered architecture recommendation stands, but with one important addition:

**Graphify is the strongest candidate for Layer 2, and SKF should prepare for it — but not depend on it.**

Concretely:

1. **Layer 0 (ship now)**: Persistent source cache + CCC index preservation. Zero new dependencies.
2. **Layer 1 (ship now)**: Registry with tool-agnostic metadata slots. Design the registry so a future graphify integration can register its outputs without changing the registry schema.
3. **Layer 2 (ship when graphify stabilizes)**: `graphify` as an optional workspace tenant. The workspace directory layout already accommodates it naturally (`graphify-out/` inside each repo checkout). When graphify hits v1.0 with a stable schema, SKF adds a `workspace graphify` command.
4. **Layer 3 (experimental)**: Cross-repo graph merging for stack skills. This is the killer feature, and graphify's community detection + god nodes are the right foundation — when they're stable.

**The 16.6k stars tell me graphify will survive. The 10-releases-today velocity tells me it won't stabilize for months.** SKF should design the workspace to welcome graphify when it's ready, not bet on it now.

---

Now let me capture the new ideas from this research:

**[Research #12]**: Graphify Accommodation Pattern
_Concept_: Design the workspace so `graphify-out/` directories are naturally preserved inside repo checkouts. No graphify-specific code needed — just don't delete the checkout. When graphify stabilizes, SKF adds awareness of god nodes and communities to forge intelligence.
_Novelty_: Passive accommodation costs nothing and preserves optionality. Active integration can wait.

**[Research #13]**: Tool Maturity Gate for Layer 2 Tenants
_Concept_: Define criteria for when a tool graduates from "user can use it" to "SKF integrates with it": stable schema (versioned), 6+ months of releases, no critical security fixes in last 3 months, deletion handling. Graphify isn't there yet. CCC is.
_Novelty_: Turns tool adoption into a principled decision rather than hype-driven.

**[Research #14]**: Registry Future-Proofing for Graph Data
_Concept_: Include an extensible `tools` section in `registry.json` where any tool can register its state. E.g., `{"tools": {"ccc": {"indexed_at": "...", "files": 1200}, "graphify": {"graph_nodes": 285, "built_at": "..."}}}`. No tool-specific code needed — just a key-value store.
_Novelty_: The registry becomes the workspace's "knowledge about its own knowledge" without coupling to any tool.

**[Research #15]**: GitNexus as Alternative Watch
_Concept_: GitNexus (14k stars, deep MCP integration, 7 tools) is the main competitor but uses PolyForm Noncommercial license. Worth watching as a backup if graphify stalls. CodeGraphContext (MIT, 2.2k stars, 100k downloads) is the safe commercial alternative.
_Novelty_: Having identified alternatives reduces single-tool risk for the Layer 2 decision.

---

That's 15 ideas from the First Principles phase. We've established the fundamental architecture: **layered workspace, tool-agnostic persistence, graphify as a future tenant not a foundation.**

**Ready to move to Phase 2 — Morphological Analysis?** This is where we'll systematically map every design parameter and explore combinations. The big questions: staleness strategy, cleanup policy, sparse checkout vs full, registry schema, installer integration, multi-ref handling.

content.text:
{
  "markdown": "[Skip to main content](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

![cover](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/assets/images/cover-74fc64b516b3870ac080d98f4b74110e.png)

Many developer tools need a long-running local process — an LSP server, a file watcher, an indexing service. The challenge isn't just building the daemon. It's making it invisible: something that starts when needed, upgrades when the tool upgrades, reloads config when settings change, and shuts down cleanly.

We learned this building [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-code/cocoindex-code), an embedded AST-based semantic code search tool that speeds up and improve the quality for coding agents. It started as a monolithic process that loaded an ML model, indexed a codebase, and served searches — all per session. We moved the heavy lifting to a persistent daemon, with a CLI (`ccc`) and a lightweight MCP client as thin frontends. This also unlocked a benefit beyond performance: humans can call `ccc search "auth logic"` directly from the terminal, piped into other tools.

The journey from "obvious daemon" to production-ready was full of design decisions — and the best ones were usually about what to _remove_. Here's the user journey:

```bash
ccc init                    # one-time setup: creates settings files (no daemon)
ccc index                   # build the search index (daemon auto-starts here)
ccc search "auth logic"     # semantic search (daemon handles it)
ccc search --refresh "auth" # re-index then search (two daemon requests)
```

`ccc init` is purely local — it creates configuration files and a `.gitignore` entry. Every other command talks to the daemon. But the user never starts, stops, or restarts the daemon manually. It just works.

The initial daemon was largely built by Claude Code — persistent connections, async handler loops, multi-step shutdown. It worked, but it was complex in ways we didn't appreciate until we looked closely. The simplifications came from stepping back and questioning the design rather than fixing individual bugs.

This post covers the patterns we landed on, in the hope that they're useful to anyone building a local daemon for developer tools.

## The Invisible Daemon [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#the-invisible-daemon "Direct link to The Invisible Daemon")

The most important design constraint: **users should never think about the daemon**. No `ccc daemon start` in the setup guide. No "please restart the daemon after upgrading." No stale config surprises.

### Start on First Use [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#start-on-first-use "Direct link to Start on First Use")

The first time a user runs `ccc index` or `ccc search`, the client tries to connect to the daemon's Unix socket. If it fails, it starts the daemon as a detached background process and waits for the socket to appear.

This logic is built into the lowest-level connection function, not into a separate "ensure daemon" step:

```python
_daemon_ensured = False

def _connect_and_handshake() -> Connection:
    global _daemon_ensured

    if _daemon_ensured:
        return _raw_connect_and_handshake()

    # First connection — auto-start/restart as needed.
    try:
        conn = _raw_connect_and_handshake()
        _daemon_ensured = True
        return conn
    except DaemonVersionError:
        stop_daemon()
    except (ConnectionRefusedError, OSError):
        pass

    start_daemon()
    _wait_for_daemon()
    # ... retry and return
```

A module-level `_daemon_ensured` flag means the first call in a CLI session pays the startup cost. Every subsequent call connects directly. No separate "ensure" step, no throwaway probe connection, no wrapper function.

### Transparent Upgrades via Version Handshake [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#transparent-upgrades-via-version-handshake "Direct link to Transparent Upgrades via Version Handshake")

When a user runs `pipx upgrade cocoindex-code`, the next CLI command should just work — using the new version. No manual restart.

Every connection starts with a version handshake. The client sends its version; the daemon responds with its version:

```python
# In the client
conn.send_bytes(encode_request(HandshakeRequest(version=__version__)))
resp = decode_response(conn.recv_bytes())
if not resp.ok or _needs_restart(resp):
    raise DaemonVersionError(resp)
```

On the first call (`_daemon_ensured = False`), a version mismatch triggers a restart — stop the old daemon, start a new one. On subsequent calls, a mismatch raises an error (the daemon was replaced mid-session, which is unusual and worth surfacing).

### Two-Level Settings, Two Strategies [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#two-level-settings-two-strategies "Direct link to Two-Level Settings, Two Strategies")

![Two-Level Settings](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/assets/images/two-level-settings-4c23d5497ee8f07393be4472b38269b0.png)

We have two levels of configuration:

**Global settings** (`~/.cocoindex_code/global_settings.yml`) — embedding model, API keys, environment variables. These affect daemon-wide state: the ML model is loaded once at startup, environment variables are set once. When these change, the daemon _must_ restart.

**Project settings** (`$PROJECT/.cocoindex_code/settings.yml`) — include/exclude patterns, language overrides. These only affect per-operation behavior: which files to index, how to parse them.

Both files are created automatically by `ccc init` with sensible defaults — users only edit them if they need to customize.

For global settings, the daemon records the file's mtime (as integer microseconds) at startup and reports it in the handshake response. The client compares it with the current mtime. Different? Restart.

For project settings, we do nothing special. Each indexing pass reads the settings file fresh from disk. No staleness check, no restart, no cache invalidation.

The principle: **minimize what the daemon holds as state**. Global settings are unavoidable state (the loaded model), so we detect staleness. Project settings can be stateless, so we make them stateless.

## Per-Request Connections [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#per-request-connections "Direct link to Per-Request Connections")

This was the single biggest simplification, and it came from a bug.

### The Persistent Connection Attempt [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#the-persistent-connection-attempt "Direct link to The Persistent Connection Attempt")

Our first design followed the obvious pattern: the client connects, handshakes, then sends multiple requests on the same connection. The daemon handler loops, reading and dispatching requests until the client disconnects:

```python
# The original handler — persistent connection with recv loop
async def handle_connection(conn, registry, ...):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

    def _recv():
        while not shutdown_event.is_set():
            if conn.poll(0.5):          # wake every 0.5s to check shutdown
                return conn.recv_bytes()
        raise EOFError("shutdown")

    try:
        while not shutdown_event.is_set():
            data = await loop.run_in_executor(None, _recv)
            req = decode_request(data)
            # ... handshake, dispatch, respond ...
    except (EOFError, OSError, asyncio.CancelledError):
        pass
    finally:
        conn.close()
```

This handler had to deal with a lot:

- A `_recv` helper that polls every 0.5 seconds, checking a `threading.Event` for shutdown
- An executor thread per connection, blocked in `conn.poll()`
- A `while` loop waiting for the next request that may never come
- Cancellation handling for shutdown

Two problems emerged from this design:

**Leaked connections.** CLI commands didn't always close their connections. Each leaked connection meant a handler task stuck in `_recv`, waiting forever. We added `__enter__`/`__exit__`, `with` blocks, `.close()` calls — but it was whack-a-mole.

**Shutdown was broken.** Our test suite took 15 seconds per test teardown. The `stop_daemon()` function had a 5-step escalation — StopRequest, wait 5 seconds, SIGTERM, wait 2 seconds, SIGKILL — and always fell through to SIGKILL. Three bugs were stacked on top of each other:

1. `asyncio.Event.is_set()` called from executor threads — but `asyncio.Event` is not thread-safe. The `_recv` threads never saw the shutdown signal.
2. `asyncio.run()` cleanup hanging — it calls `shutdown_default_executor(wait=True)`, which waits for the `_recv` threads that would never exit (because of bug #1).
3. `os.kill(pid, 0)` returning True for zombie processes — so the client kept waiting for a process that had already exited.

We fixed each bug individually. But the real insight came when we stepped back: **all three bugs existed because of persistent connections**. Persistent connections required idle handler tasks. Idle tasks required a shutdown signal. The signal had to cross async-to-thread boundaries. And every layer of that chain had a bug.

### The Per-Request Model [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#the-per-request-model "Direct link to The Per-Request Model")

Instead of fixing the plumbing, we removed the need for it. What if each request just opens a fresh connection?

```text
connect → handshake → one request → response(s) → close
```

The handler becomes straight-line code:

```python
# The current handler — per-request, no loop, no polling
async def handle_connection(conn, registry, ...):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    try:
        # 1. Handshake
        data = await loop.run_in_executor(None, conn.recv_bytes)
        req = decode_request(data)
        # ... validate handshake, send response ...

        # 2. Single request
        data = await loop.run_in_executor(None, conn.recv_bytes)
        req = decode_request(data)
        result = await _dispatch(req, registry, ...)

        # 3. Send response(s) and done
        if isinstance(result, AsyncIterator):
            async for resp in result:
                conn.send_bytes(encode_response(resp))
        else:
            conn.send_bytes(encode_response(result))
    except (EOFError, OSError, asyncio.CancelledError):
        pass
    finally:
        conn.close()
```

Read two messages. Respond. Close. That's it.

### What It Eliminated [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#what-it-eliminated "Direct link to What It Eliminated")

The diff wasn't just shorter — entire concepts disappeared:

- The `_recv` polling loop (0.5-second wakeups checking `shutdown_event`) — **gone**
- `shutdown_event` (`threading.Event`) — **gone entirely**
- The `DaemonClient` class, `__enter__`/`__exit__`, `.close()` — **gone**
- `with client:` blocks in every CLI command — **gone**
- Connection leak bugs — **impossible by construction**
- All three shutdown bugs — **gone** (no idle handler tasks means nothing to signal)

The client module went from a stateful class to module-level functions:

```python
# No class, no state, no close() to forget
def index(project_root, on_progress=None, on_waiting=None):
    conn = _connect_and_handshake()
    try:
        conn.send_bytes(encode_request(IndexRequest(project_root=project_root)))
        # ... read streaming responses ...
    finally:
        conn.close()
```

### Compound Operations [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#compound-operations "Direct link to Compound Operations")

What about operations that need multiple requests? `ccc search --refresh` needs to index first, then search. Simple — two connections:

```python
def search_cmd(project_root, refresh=False):
    if refresh:
        client.index(project_root)       # connection 1
    client.search(project_root, query)   # connection 2
```

MCP background indexing? Just call `client.index()` from a thread — it opens its own connection. No shared state, no "dedicated connection" comments, no connection isolation bugs.

The overhead is negligible: Unix socket setup is ~0.1ms, the handshake is a tiny msgspec payload, and CLI commands are human-initiated. Streaming responses (like indexing progress) still work — the connection lives for one request's full response stream, it just doesn't linger afterwards.

## Shutdown: Close Resources, Don't Signal Threads [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#shutdown-close-resources-dont-signal-threads "Direct link to Shutdown: Close Resources, Don't Signal Threads")

Per-request connections solved the hardest shutdown problems (no idle tasks to signal). The remaining lesson: **design shutdown around closing resources, not coordinating threads**.

The accept loop runs in a background thread, blocking on `listener.accept()`. To stop it, we don't set a flag and hope the thread notices — we close the listener. `accept()` raises `OSError`, the thread exits immediately:

```python
def _accept_loop():
    while True:
        try:
            conn = listener.accept()
            loop.call_soon_threadsafe(_spawn_handler, conn)
        except OSError:
            break  # listener was closed — exit
```

The same principle applies to the full shutdown sequence:

```python
try:
    loop.run_forever()
finally:
    listener.close()                    # 1. stop accepting
    for task in tasks: task.cancel()    # 2. cancel handlers
    if tasks:
        loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True))
    registry.close_all()                # 3. release resources
    loop.close()
    # 4. remove socket and PID file ...
    os._exit(0)                         # 5. skip slow Python teardown
```

A few details worth noting:

**PID file as the exit signal.** The client needs to know when the daemon has finished cleaning up. We tried `os.kill(pid, 0)`, but it returns True for zombie processes (they're still in the process table). Instead, the daemon removes its PID file as the last cleanup step, and the client polls for its absence.

**`os._exit(0)` for fast teardown.** Python's normal exit runs atexit handlers, GC finalizers, and module teardown — which takes seconds when torch is loaded. Since we've already cleaned up explicitly, `os._exit(0)` is safe and fast. (One caveat: it terminates the _entire process_, so we guard it with `threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()` for tests that run the daemon in a thread.)

**Event loop on the main thread.** The accept loop runs in a background thread, but the asyncio event loop stays on the main thread. This is required for `loop.add_signal_handler()` to work — SIGTERM and SIGINT call `loop.stop()`, which causes `run_forever()` to return into the `finally` block above.

## The Result [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#the-result "Direct link to The Result")

```text
                    per-request connections
  CLI / MCP  ──────────────────────────────►  Daemon
  (thin)        connect → handshake →         (persistent)
                request → response →          - event loop (main thread)
                close                         - accept loop (background thread)
                                              - project registry
                                              - ML model (loaded once)
```

Before and after:

|  | Before | After |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Connection handler | ~70 lines, recv loop, polling, shutdown event | ~40 lines, straight-line read-respond-close |
| Client | `DaemonClient` class, `__enter__`/`__exit__`, `.close()` | Module-level functions, no state |
| Shutdown concepts | `threading.Event`, executor polling, 5-step escalation | `listener.close()`, `loop.stop()`, `os._exit(0)` |
| Daemon stop time | ~15 seconds (fell through to SIGKILL) | <1 second |

## Patterns for Local Daemon Architecture [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#patterns-for-local-daemon-architecture "Direct link to Patterns for Local Daemon Architecture")

If you're building a local daemon for developer tools, here's what we'd suggest:

**Make the daemon invisible.** Auto-start on first use. Auto-restart on version mismatch. Auto-reload config where possible. The user's mental model should be "I run CLI commands" — the daemon is an implementation detail.

**Prefer stateless IPC.** Per-request connections can't leak by construction. The overhead of Unix socket setup (~0.1ms) is negligible for developer tools. The handshake on every connection catches staleness faster.

**Categorize your state.** What _must_ live in the daemon (loaded ML model, open databases) vs what can be read fresh (config files, file lists)? Only restart for the former. The less state the daemon holds, the fewer staleness bugs you'll have.

**Design shutdown around resource closure, not signaling.** Close the listener to interrupt `accept()`. Remove the PID file to signal completion. Don't poll threads to check if they've noticed an event — close the thing they're waiting on.

**When debugging gets complex, question the design.** We fixed three stacked shutdown bugs with careful threading, event types, and process liveness checks. Then we removed persistent connections and all three bugs disappeared. The debugging was correct. The design was wrong.

## Support the Project [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#support-the-project "Direct link to Support the Project")

If you find this article helpful, please check out [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-io/cocoindex-code) for the implementation and star the project if you like it!

![cocoindex-code](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/assets/images/cocoindex-code-b484effbcd5647b433b2d80f081d4cab.png)

- [The Invisible Daemon](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#the-invisible-daemon)
  - [Start on First Use](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#start-on-first-use)
  - [Transparent Upgrades via Version Handshake](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#transparent-upgrades-via-version-handshake)
  - [Two-Level Settings, Two Strategies](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#two-level-settings-two-strategies)
- [Per-Request Connections](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#per-request-connections)
  - [The Persistent Connection Attempt](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#the-persistent-connection-attempt)
  - [The Per-Request Model](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#the-per-request-model)
  - [What It Eliminated](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#what-it-eliminated)
  - [Compound Operations](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#compound-operations)
- [Shutdown: Close Resources, Don't Signal Threads](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#shutdown-close-resources-dont-signal-threads)

{
  "markdown": "[Skip to main content](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

![cover](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/assets/images/cover-74fc64b516b3870ac080d98f4b74110e.png)

Many developer tools need a long-running local process — an LSP server, a file watcher, an indexing service. The challenge isn't just building the daemon. It's making it invisible: something that starts when needed, upgrades when the tool upgrades, reloads config when settings change, and shuts down cleanly.

We learned this building [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-code/cocoindex-code), an embedded AST-based semantic code search tool that speeds up and improve the quality for coding agents. It started as a monolithic process that loaded an ML model, indexed a codebase, and served searches — all per session. We moved the heavy lifting to a persistent daemon, with a CLI (`ccc`) and a lightweight MCP client as thin frontends. This also unlocked a benefit beyond performance: humans can call `ccc search "auth logic"` directly from the terminal, piped into other tools.

The journey from "obvious daemon" to production-ready was full of design decisions — and the best ones were usually about what to _remove_. Here's the user journey:

```bash
ccc init                    # one-time setup: creates settings files (no daemon)
ccc index                   # build the search index (daemon auto-starts here)
ccc search "auth logic"     # semantic search (daemon handles it)
ccc search --refresh "auth" # re-index then search (two daemon requests)
```

`ccc init` is purely local — it creates configuration files and a `.gitignore` entry. Every other command talks to the daemon. But the user never starts, stops, or restarts the daemon manually. It just works.

The initial daemon was largely built by Claude Code — persistent connections, async handler loops, multi-step shutdown. It worked, but it was complex in ways we didn't appreciate until we looked closely. The simplifications came from stepping back and questioning the design rather than fixing individual bugs.

This post covers the patterns we landed on, in the hope that they're useful to anyone building a local daemon for developer tools.

## The Invisible Daemon [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#the-invisible-daemon "Direct link to The Invisible Daemon")

The most important design constraint: **users should never think about the daemon**. No `ccc daemon start` in the setup guide. No "please restart the daemon after upgrading." No stale config surprises.

### Start on First Use [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#start-on-first-use "Direct link to Start on First Use")

The first time a user runs `ccc index` or `ccc search`, the client tries to connect to the daemon's Unix socket. If it fails, it starts the daemon as a detached background process and waits for the socket to appear.

This logic is built into the lowest-level connection function, not into a separate "ensure daemon" step:

```python
_daemon_ensured = False

def _connect_and_handshake() -> Connection:
    global _daemon_ensured

    if _daemon_ensured:
        return _raw_connect_and_handshake()

    # First connection — auto-start/restart as needed.
    try:
        conn = _raw_connect_and_handshake()
        _daemon_ensured = True
        return conn
    except DaemonVersionError:
        stop_daemon()
    except (ConnectionRefusedError, OSError):
        pass

    start_daemon()
    _wait_for_daemon()
    # ... retry and return
```

A module-level `_daemon_ensured` flag means the first call in a CLI session pays the startup cost. Every subsequent call connects directly. No separate "ensure" step, no throwaway probe connection, no wrapper function.

### Transparent Upgrades via Version Handshake [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#transparent-upgrades-via-version-handshake "Direct link to Transparent Upgrades via Version Handshake")

When a user runs `pipx upgrade cocoindex-code`, the next CLI command should just work — using the new version. No manual restart.

Every connection starts with a version handshake. The client sends its version; the daemon responds with its version:

```python
# In the client
conn.send_bytes(encode_request(HandshakeRequest(version=__version__)))
resp = decode_response(conn.recv_bytes())
if not resp.ok or _needs_restart(resp):
    raise DaemonVersionError(resp)
```

On the first call (`_daemon_ensured = False`), a version mismatch triggers a restart — stop the old daemon, start a new one. On subsequent calls, a mismatch raises an error (the daemon was replaced mid-session, which is unusual and worth surfacing).

### Two-Level Settings, Two Strategies [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#two-level-settings-two-strategies "Direct link to Two-Level Settings, Two Strategies")

![Two-Level Settings](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/assets/images/two-level-settings-4c23d5497ee8f07393be4472b38269b0.png)

We have two levels of configuration:

**Global settings** (`~/.cocoindex_code/global_settings.yml`) — embedding model, API keys, environment variables. These affect daemon-wide state: the ML model is loaded once at startup, environment variables are set once. When these change, the daemon _must_ restart.

**Project settings** (`$PROJECT/.cocoindex_code/settings.yml`) — include/exclude patterns, language overrides. These only affect per-operation behavior: which files to index, how to parse them.

Both files are created automatically by `ccc init` with sensible defaults — users only edit them if they need to customize.

For global settings, the daemon records the file's mtime (as integer microseconds) at startup and reports it in the handshake response. The client compares it with the current mtime. Different? Restart.

For project settings, we do nothing special. Each indexing pass reads the settings file fresh from disk. No staleness check, no restart, no cache invalidation.

The principle: **minimize what the daemon holds as state**. Global settings are unavoidable state (the loaded model), so we detect staleness. Project settings can be stateless, so we make them stateless.

## Per-Request Connections [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#per-request-connections "Direct link to Per-Request Connections")

This was the single biggest simplification, and it came from a bug.

### The Persistent Connection Attempt [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#the-persistent-connection-attempt "Direct link to The Persistent Connection Attempt")

Our first design followed the obvious pattern: the client connects, handshakes, then sends multiple requests on the same connection. The daemon handler loops, reading and dispatching requests until the client disconnects:

```python
# The original handler — persistent connection with recv loop
async def handle_connection(conn, registry, ...):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

    def _recv():
        while not shutdown_event.is_set():
            if conn.poll(0.5):          # wake every 0.5s to check shutdown
                return conn.recv_bytes()
        raise EOFError("shutdown")

    try:
        while not shutdown_event.is_set():
            data = await loop.run_in_executor(None, _recv)
            req = decode_request(data)
            # ... handshake, dispatch, respond ...
    except (EOFError, OSError, asyncio.CancelledError):
        pass
    finally:
        conn.close()
```

This handler had to deal with a lot:

- A `_recv` helper that polls every 0.5 seconds, checking a `threading.Event` for shutdown
- An executor thread per connection, blocked in `conn.poll()`
- A `while` loop waiting for the next request that may never come
- Cancellation handling for shutdown

Two problems emerged from this design:

**Leaked connections.** CLI commands didn't always close their connections. Each leaked connection meant a handler task stuck in `_recv`, waiting forever. We added `__enter__`/`__exit__`, `with` blocks, `.close()` calls — but it was whack-a-mole.

**Shutdown was broken.** Our test suite took 15 seconds per test teardown. The `stop_daemon()` function had a 5-step escalation — StopRequest, wait 5 seconds, SIGTERM, wait 2 seconds, SIGKILL — and always fell through to SIGKILL. Three bugs were stacked on top of each other:

1. `asyncio.Event.is_set()` called from executor threads — but `asyncio.Event` is not thread-safe. The `_recv` threads never saw the shutdown signal.
2. `asyncio.run()` cleanup hanging — it calls `shutdown_default_executor(wait=True)`, which waits for the `_recv` threads that would never exit (because of bug #1).
3. `os.kill(pid, 0)` returning True for zombie processes — so the client kept waiting for a process that had already exited.

We fixed each bug individually. But the real insight came when we stepped back: **all three bugs existed because of persistent connections**. Persistent connections required idle handler tasks. Idle tasks required a shutdown signal. The signal had to cross async-to-thread boundaries. And every layer of that chain had a bug.

### The Per-Request Model [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#the-per-request-model "Direct link to The Per-Request Model")

Instead of fixing the plumbing, we removed the need for it. What if each request just opens a fresh connection?

```text
connect → handshake → one request → response(s) → close
```

The handler becomes straight-line code:

```python
# The current handler — per-request, no loop, no polling
async def handle_connection(conn, registry, ...):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    try:
        # 1. Handshake
        data = await loop.run_in_executor(None, conn.recv_bytes)
        req = decode_request(data)
        # ... validate handshake, send response ...

        # 2. Single request
        data = await loop.run_in_executor(None, conn.recv_bytes)
        req = decode_request(data)
        result = await _dispatch(req, registry, ...)

        # 3. Send response(s) and done
        if isinstance(result, AsyncIterator):
            async for resp in result:
                conn.send_bytes(encode_response(resp))
        else:
            conn.send_bytes(encode_response(result))
    except (EOFError, OSError, asyncio.CancelledError):
        pass
    finally:
        conn.close()
```

Read two messages. Respond. Close. That's it.

### What It Eliminated [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#what-it-eliminated "Direct link to What It Eliminated")

The diff wasn't just shorter — entire concepts disappeared:

- The `_recv` polling loop (0.5-second wakeups checking `shutdown_event`) — **gone**
- `shutdown_event` (`threading.Event`) — **gone entirely**
- The `DaemonClient` class, `__enter__`/`__exit__`, `.close()` — **gone**
- `with client:` blocks in every CLI command — **gone**
- Connection leak bugs — **impossible by construction**
- All three shutdown bugs — **gone** (no idle handler tasks means nothing to signal)

The client module went from a stateful class to module-level functions:

```python
# No class, no state, no close() to forget
def index(project_root, on_progress=None, on_waiting=None):
    conn = _connect_and_handshake()
    try:
        conn.send_bytes(encode_request(IndexRequest(project_root=project_root)))
        # ... read streaming responses ...
    finally:
        conn.close()
```

### Compound Operations [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#compound-operations "Direct link to Compound Operations")

What about operations that need multiple requests? `ccc search --refresh` needs to index first, then search. Simple — two connections:

```python
def search_cmd(project_root, refresh=False):
    if refresh:
        client.index(project_root)       # connection 1
    client.search(project_root, query)   # connection 2
```

MCP background indexing? Just call `client.index()` from a thread — it opens its own connection. No shared state, no "dedicated connection" comments, no connection isolation bugs.

The overhead is negligible: Unix socket setup is ~0.1ms, the handshake is a tiny msgspec payload, and CLI commands are human-initiated. Streaming responses (like indexing progress) still work — the connection lives for one request's full response stream, it just doesn't linger afterwards.

## Shutdown: Close Resources, Don't Signal Threads [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#shutdown-close-resources-dont-signal-threads "Direct link to Shutdown: Close Resources, Don't Signal Threads")

Per-request connections solved the hardest shutdown problems (no idle tasks to signal). The remaining lesson: **design shutdown around closing resources, not coordinating threads**.

The accept loop runs in a background thread, blocking on `listener.accept()`. To stop it, we don't set a flag and hope the thread notices — we close the listener. `accept()` raises `OSError`, the thread exits immediately:

```python
def _accept_loop():
    while True:
        try:
            conn = listener.accept()
            loop.call_soon_threadsafe(_spawn_handler, conn)
        except OSError:
            break  # listener was closed — exit
```

The same principle applies to the full shutdown sequence:

```python
try:
    loop.run_forever()
finally:
    listener.close()                    # 1. stop accepting
    for task in tasks: task.cancel()    # 2. cancel handlers
    if tasks:
        loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True))
    registry.close_all()                # 3. release resources
    loop.close()
    # 4. remove socket and PID file ...
    os._exit(0)                         # 5. skip slow Python teardown
```

A few details worth noting:

**PID file as the exit signal.** The client needs to know when the daemon has finished cleaning up. We tried `os.kill(pid, 0)`, but it returns True for zombie processes (they're still in the process table). Instead, the daemon removes its PID file as the last cleanup step, and the client polls for its absence.

**`os._exit(0)` for fast teardown.** Python's normal exit runs atexit handlers, GC finalizers, and module teardown — which takes seconds when torch is loaded. Since we've already cleaned up explicitly, `os._exit(0)` is safe and fast. (One caveat: it terminates the _entire process_, so we guard it with `threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()` for tests that run the daemon in a thread.)

**Event loop on the main thread.** The accept loop runs in a background thread, but the asyncio event loop stays on the main thread. This is required for `loop.add_signal_handler()` to work — SIGTERM and SIGINT call `loop.stop()`, which causes `run_forever()` to return into the `finally` block above.

## The Result [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#the-result "Direct link to The Result")

```text
                    per-request connections
  CLI / MCP  ──────────────────────────────►  Daemon
  (thin)        connect → handshake →         (persistent)
                request → response →          - event loop (main thread)
                close                         - accept loop (background thread)
                                              - project registry
                                              - ML model (loaded once)
```

Before and after:

|  | Before | After |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Connection handler | ~70 lines, recv loop, polling, shutdown event | ~40 lines, straight-line read-respond-close |
| Client | `DaemonClient` class, `__enter__`/`__exit__`, `.close()` | Module-level functions, no state |
| Shutdown concepts | `threading.Event`, executor polling, 5-step escalation | `listener.close()`, `loop.stop()`, `os._exit(0)` |
| Daemon stop time | ~15 seconds (fell through to SIGKILL) | <1 second |

## Patterns for Local Daemon Architecture [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#patterns-for-local-daemon-architecture "Direct link to Patterns for Local Daemon Architecture")

If you're building a local daemon for developer tools, here's what we'd suggest:

**Make the daemon invisible.** Auto-start on first use. Auto-restart on version mismatch. Auto-reload config where possible. The user's mental model should be "I run CLI commands" — the daemon is an implementation detail.

**Prefer stateless IPC.** Per-request connections can't leak by construction. The overhead of Unix socket setup (~0.1ms) is negligible for developer tools. The handshake on every connection catches staleness faster.

**Categorize your state.** What _must_ live in the daemon (loaded ML model, open databases) vs what can be read fresh (config files, file lists)? Only restart for the former. The less state the daemon holds, the fewer staleness bugs you'll have.

**Design shutdown around resource closure, not signaling.** Close the listener to interrupt `accept()`. Remove the PID file to signal completion. Don't poll threads to check if they've noticed an event — close the thing they're waiting on.

**When debugging gets complex, question the design.** We fixed three stacked shutdown bugs with careful threading, event types, and process liveness checks. Then we removed persistent connections and all three bugs disappeared. The debugging was correct. The design was wrong.

## Support the Project [​](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon\#support-the-project "Direct link to Support the Project")

If you find this article helpful, please check out [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-io/cocoindex-code) for the implementation and star the project if you like it!

![cocoindex-code](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/assets/images/cocoindex-code-b484effbcd5647b433b2d80f081d4cab.png)

- [The Invisible Daemon](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#the-invisible-daemon)
  - [Start on First Use](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#start-on-first-use)
  - [Transparent Upgrades via Version Handshake](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#transparent-upgrades-via-version-handshake)
  - [Two-Level Settings, Two Strategies](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#two-level-settings-two-strategies)
- [Per-Request Connections](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#per-request-connections)
  - [The Persistent Connection Attempt](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#the-persistent-connection-attempt)
  - [The Per-Request Model](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#the-per-request-model)
  - [What It Eliminated](https://cocoindex.io/blogs/building-an-invisible-daemon#what-it-eliminated)

content.text:
This is a critical technical insight that simplifies the workspace design significantly. Let me capture what it means:

---

## CCC Daemon Architecture — Impact on Workspace Design

**Key revelation: CCC already runs as a system-level daemon.**

| Property | CCC Daemon Behavior | Workspace Implication |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Auto-start** | First `ccc index` or `ccc search` starts the daemon transparently | Workspace doesn't need to manage daemon lifecycle at all |
| **Per-request connections** | Each command opens a fresh Unix socket connection with `project_root` as parameter | Workspace repos are just paths passed to `ccc index {path}` |
| **Project registry** | Daemon internally maintains a registry of project roots → indexes | Workspace repos auto-register as additional "projects" |
| **ML model loaded once** | Embedding model loaded at daemon startup, shared across all projects | First workspace repo pays startup cost, subsequent repos are near-instant |
| **Two-level settings** | Global: `~/.cocoindex_code/global_settings.yml`, Project: `{root}/.cocoindex_code/settings.yml` | Each workspace repo gets its own project settings; global settings shared |
| **Index persistence** | `.cocoindex_code/` directory inside the project root | Lives naturally inside the repo checkout — **already persistent** |
| **Auto-upgrade** | Version handshake on every connection; mismatch → auto-restart | Workspace inherits this for free |

**The punchline:** The workspace doesn't need any CCC-specific integration code. It just needs to:
1. Keep the repo checkout at `~/.skf-workspace/repos/{host}/{owner}/{repo}/`
2. Call `ccc index {that_path}` — daemon auto-starts, registers the project, builds index
3. Call `ccc search {query}` from that path — daemon serves from the persisted index
4. On re-forge: call `ccc search --refresh {query}` — re-indexes if stale, then searches

**The CCC index at `.cocoindex_code/` persists inside the checkout. The daemon auto-discovers it. No glue code needed.**

---

This changes the complexity estimate for Layer 0 dramatically:

**[Architecture #16]**: Zero-Glue CCC Integration
_Concept_: Because CCC's daemon is already system-level with a project registry, the workspace gets persistent indexing for free just by preserving the checkout directory. The source-resolution protocol only needs to change the path from `/tmp/skf-ephemeral-{name}-{ts}/` to `~/.skf-workspace/repos/{host}/{owner}/{repo}/` — everything downstream (ccc init, ccc index, ccc search) works unchanged.
_Novelty_: What looked like a complex integration problem (persistent CCC indexes) is actually a one-line path change. The daemon architecture already solved persistence.

**[Architecture #17]**: Daemon-Aware Staleness
_Concept_: CCC's `--refresh` flag on search already handles "re-index then search" as a compound operation (two daemon connections). The workspace staleness check becomes: `git fetch` to update files → `ccc search --refresh` to re-index if files changed. CCC handles the "did files actually change?" logic internally.
_Novelty_: Staleness for indexing is delegated to CCC's own change detection, not reimplemented by the workspace.

**[Architecture #18]**: Shared Daemon, Multiplied Value
_Concept_: The CCC daemon loads its ML model once (~seconds). With ephemeral cloning, this cost was paid per-session. With a persistent workspace, the daemon stays warm across forges, across projects, across sessions. Ten forges from ten repos share one model load.
_Novelty_: The workspace doesn't just avoid re-cloning — it keeps the daemon's expensive state (loaded model) warm and amortized.

---

That's 18 ideas. The First Principles phase has yielded a clear, simple architecture:

**Core insight: The workspace is mostly just "don't delete the checkout." CCC's daemon architecture already provides persistent indexing, auto-lifecycle, and cross-project sharing. Graphify and future tools can ride the same pattern — persist their outputs inside the checkout, let their own runtimes manage lifecycle.**

Ready for **Phase 2 — Morphological Analysis**? Now we map the specific parameter space: registry schema, staleness thresholds, cleanup policy, sparse checkout handling, installer integration, multi-ref scenarios, error recovery. This is where the design gets concrete.

input.content:
# Graphify x SKF — Contribution Brief & PRD

**Author:** Armel (SKF maintainer)
**Date:** 2026-04-09
**Status:** Draft — for discussion with graphify maintainer
**Context:** SKF (Skill Forge) is building a persistent workspace (`~/.skf-workspace`) that caches remote repo clones and their tool indexes across sessions. Graphify is the strongest candidate for the knowledge graph layer in this workspace.

---

## 1. Executive Summary

SKF forges AI agent skills from source code repositories. We're building a persistent, system-level workspace that replaces expensive ephemeral git cloning with a shared source cache. The workspace architecture has a layered design where knowledge graph tools like graphify can persist their outputs alongside the source trees.

We want to contribute to graphify to help it mature toward the stability and features SKF needs — benefiting the broader graphify ecosystem, not just SKF.

---

## 2. SKF's Vision for Knowledge Graphs

### 2.1 The Stack Skill Problem

SKF can forge skills from individual repos. But "stack skills" (skills that span multiple repos — e.g., a full-stack skill covering a frontend framework + backend framework + database) currently require manual composition. A cross-repo knowledge graph could **discover** stack relationships automatically:

- God nodes that appear across repos (shared abstractions, common patterns)
- Surprising connections between repos (repo-A implements a pattern that repo-B's middleware expects)
- Community detection across a merged multi-repo graph (architectural layers that span repos)

### 2.2 The Workspace Architecture

```
~/.skf-workspace/
├── repos/
│   └── github.com/
│       └── facebook/
│           └── react/
│               ├── .git/
│               ├── .cocoindex_code/    # CCC index (persisted)
│               ├── graphify-out/       # Graphify artifacts (persisted)
│               │   ├── graph.json
│               │   ├── cache/
│               │   ├── GRAPH_REPORT.md
│               │   └── graph.html
│               └── src/
├── _merged/                            # Future: cross-repo merged graphs
├── registry.json
└── config.yaml
```

Graphify's `graphify-out/` directory naturally persists inside each repo checkout. The SHA256 cache makes re-runs incremental. This is already a great fit.

---

## 3. What SKF Needs (Prioritized)

### P0 — Required for Layer 2 Integration

#### 3.1 Schema Versioning for graph.json
- **Current state:** graph.json schema is unversioned, based on NetworkX node_link_data format
- **Need:** A `schema_version` field in graph.json so consumers can detect breaking changes
- **Why:** SKF reads graph.json programmatically. Without versioning, a graphify upgrade can silently break SKF's graph queries
- **Proposed:** Add `{"schema_version": "1.0", "nodes": [...], "links": [...]}` at the top level

#### 3.2 File Deletion Handling in Incremental Updates
- **Current state:** Deleted files leave ghost nodes/edges in graph.json. Only full rebuild removes them.
- **Need:** `--update` should detect deleted files and remove their nodes/edges
- **Why:** In a persistent workspace, repos evolve. Ghost nodes from deleted files corrupt the graph over time.
- **Proposed:** Compare manifest files against current file tree. Remove nodes where `source_file` no longer exists. Remove orphaned edges.

#### 3.3 Cross-Platform Compatibility (Linux/macOS/Windows)
- **Current state:** Git hooks fixed for Windows in v0.3.21 (`#!/bin/sh` instead of `#!/bin/bash`). Other Windows issues may remain.
- **Need:** Full Windows support for core operations (build, query, watch, MCP server)
- **Why:** SKF targets Linux/macOS/Windows. The workspace must work on all three.
- **Proposed:** CI matrix testing on all three platforms. Path handling with `pathlib`. No Unix-only assumptions.

### P1 — Required for Layer 3 (Cross-Repo Intelligence)

#### 3.4 Graph Merging API
- **Current state:** Each `graphify-out/` is isolated per repo. No cross-repo merging.
- **Need:** A Python API or CLI command to merge multiple graph.json files into a unified graph with cross-repo edges
- **Why:** Stack skills need cross-repo relationship discovery. Graphify's Leiden community detection on a merged graph could find architectural layers spanning repos.
- **Proposed:** `graphify merge --output merged/ repo-a/graphify-out/graph.json repo-b/graphify-out/graph.json`
  - Preserve source provenance (which repo each node came from)
  - Run community detection on the merged graph
  - Identify cross-repo surprising connections

#### 3.5 Programmatic Python API (Not Just CLI/Skill)
- **Current state:** Core functionality is accessible via Python API (serve.py functions) but not formally documented as a public API
- **Need:** A stable, documented Python API for: load graph, query, get neighbors, get communities, merge graphs
- **Why:** SKF's forge pipeline is orchestrated by LLM agents that call tools programmatically. A stable API is more reliable than parsing CLI output.
- **Proposed:** `graphify.api` module with versioned, documented functions

#### 3.6 Shallow Clone Compatibility Validation
- **Current state:** Graphify works on local file trees. Shallow clones should work but aren't explicitly tested.
- **Need:** Verified support for `git clone --depth 1 --filter=blob:none` checkouts
- **Why:** Workspace repos start as shallow clones to minimize disk usage
- **Proposed:** Add shallow clone repos to the test suite

### P2 — Nice to Have (Future Enhancement)

#### 3.7 Token-Budgeted Graph Summaries for LLM Consumption
- **Current state:** MCP server has token budgets for query responses (default 2000 tokens)
- **Need:** A "graph summary" output format optimized for LLM context windows — god nodes, top communities, key surprising connections, in a structured format
- **Why:** SKF's forge pipeline injects source intelligence into the LLM's context. A pre-formatted graph summary would slot directly into the forge prompt.

#### 3.8 Incremental Watch Mode for Workspace Repos
- **Current state:** `watch.py` monitors a single folder. Code changes trigger instant AST rebuild. Doc changes set a flag for manual `--update`.
- **Need:** Watch mode that can monitor multiple repos (or a workspace root) and maintain graphs incrementally
- **Why:** The workspace holds N repos. Watching all of them with a single graphify process would be more efficient than N separate watchers.

---

## 4. What SKF Can Contribute

### 4.1 Testing
- Cross-platform CI testing (Linux/macOS/Windows) through SKF's existing test infrastructure
- Shallow clone test fixtures
- Large-repo stress testing (SKF users forge from repos with 1000+ files)

### 4.2 Real-World Usage Feedback
- SKF's forge pipeline is a demanding consumer of knowledge graphs — it needs accurate, stale-free data for skill extraction
- Edge cases from production usage: concurrent forges, multi-ref checkouts, incremental updates after git fetch

### 4.3 Graph Merging Design Input
- SKF has deep context on what "cross-repo intelligence" means in practice (stack skills, audit skills)
- Can provide real-world test cases: "merge React + Express + PostgreSQL graphs and find the stack patterns"

### 4.4 Documentation
- Usage documentation for the persistent workspace pattern
- Integration guide for tools that want to persist outputs alongside graphify

---

## 5. Integration Timeline (SKF-side)

| Phase | SKF Milestone | Graphify Dependency |
|-------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Now** | Workspace Layer 0 ships (persistent clones + CCC) | None |
| **When P0 met** | Workspace Layer 2: optional graphify integration | Schema versioning, deletion handling, Windows support |
| **When P1 met** | Workspace Layer 3: cross-repo graph merging for stack skills | Graph merging API, stable Python API |

---

## 6. Open Questions for Discussion

1. **Schema stability timeline:** When does graphify expect to stabilize the graph.json schema? Would a schema_version field be accepted now?
2. **Graph merging interest:** Is cross-repo graph merging on graphify's roadmap, or would this be a community contribution?
3. **MCP server as workspace service:** Could the MCP server be extended to serve multiple graphs (one per workspace repo) from a single process?
4. **License confirmation:** graphify is MIT — confirmed compatible with SKF's intended use?

---

*This brief is intended to start a conversation with the graphify maintainer. SKF is committed to contributing upstream rather than forking.*

name: Graphify contribution plan
description: Armel plans to contribute to safishamsi/graphify to mature it for SKF workspace Layer 2/3 integration — cross-repo knowledge graphs for stack skills
type: project
---

Armel is open to contributing to safishamsi/graphify (MIT, 16.6k stars) to help it mature for SKF integration.

**Why:** Graphify is the strongest candidate for the knowledge graph layer in the SKF persistent workspace (~/.skf-workspace). Cross-repo graph merging could enable automatic stack skill relationship discovery. However, graphify is currently pre-alpha (v0.3.x, rapid release cadence, schema unversioned, deletion handling missing).

**How to apply:** When discussing workspace features, graphify integration, or stack skills, be aware that graphify is a planned Layer 2 tenant — optional, not foundational. A contribution brief/PRD has been saved at `_bmad-output/brainstorming/graphify-skf-contribution-brief.md`. SKF workspace Layer 0 (persistent clones + CCC) ships independently of graphify readiness.

**Key gaps to close before integration:** schema versioning for graph.json, file deletion handling in incremental updates, cross-platform support (Linux/macOS/Windows), graph merging API for cross-repo intelligence, stable Python API.

12	# Brainstorming Session Results
13	
14	**Facilitator:** Armel
15	**Date:** 2026-04-09
16	
17	## Session Overview
18	
19	**Topic:** SKF Persistent Workspace Directory — a system-level directory at `~/.skf-workspace` that replaces ephemeral git cloning with persistent source caching, preserves CCC indexes across projects and sessions, and may serve broader SKF infrastructure needs not yet identified.
20	
21	**Goals:**
22	- Eliminate redundant clone + index cycles across all projects on the machine
23	- Explore what else a global SKF workspace could genuinely enable
24	- Identify breaking changes, regressions, and edge cases
25	- Produce a design solid enough for party mode multi-perspective stress testing
26	
27	### Context Guidance
28	
29	_Current architecture: Every remote source at Forge/Deep tier triggers `git clone --depth 1` into `/tmp/skf-ephemeral-{name}-{timestamp}/`, followed by `ccc init` + `ccc index`, semantic discovery via `ccc search`, then full cleanup via `rm -rf`. Re-forging the same repo re-clones and re-indexes from scratch. Key files: source-resolution-protocols.md, step-03-extract.md, step-01b-ccc-index.md, installer.js, forge-tier.yaml, module.yaml._
30	
31	### Session Setup
32	
33	_AI-recommended three-phase approach: First Principles Thinking (strip assumptions) → Morphological Analysis (map design space) → Chaos Engineering (stress test). User confirmed approach._
34	
35	## Technique Selection
36	
37	**Approach:** AI-Recommended Techniques
38	**Analysis Context:** System-level infrastructure design with cross-cutting concerns across installer, source resolution, CCC indexing, state management, and multi-project sharing
39	
40	**Recommended Techniques:**
41	
42	- **First Principles Thinking:** Strip away assumptions baked into the ephemeral model, rebuild from fundamental truths about what SKF needs from source access. Discover genuine use cases beyond clone caching.
43	- **Morphological Analysis:** Systematically map every design parameter (git strategy, staleness, layout, cleanup, CCC lifecycle, sharing) and explore combinations to prevent locking into the first obvious design.
44	- **Chaos Engineering:** Deliberately break the design — disk full, concurrent forges, upstream deletion, corrupted index — to catalog failure modes before party mode.

# Brainstorming Session Results

**Facilitator:** Armel
**Date:** 2026-04-09

## Session Overview

**Topic:** SKF Persistent Workspace Directory — a system-level directory at `~/.skf-workspace` that replaces ephemeral git cloning with persistent source caching, preserves CCC indexes across projects and sessions, and may serve broader SKF infrastructure needs not yet identified.

**Goals:**
- Eliminate redundant clone + index cycles across all projects on the machine
- Explore what else a global SKF workspace could genuinely enable
- Identify breaking changes, regressions, and edge cases
- Produce a design solid enough for party mode multi-perspective stress testing

### Context Guidance

_Current architecture: Every remote source at Forge/Deep tier triggers `git clone --depth 1` into `/tmp/skf-ephemeral-{name}-{timestamp}/`, followed by `ccc init` + `ccc index`, semantic discovery via `ccc search`, then full cleanup via `rm -rf`. Re-forging the same repo re-clones and re-indexes from scratch. Key files: source-resolution-protocols.md, step-03-extract.md, step-01b-ccc-index.md, installer.js, forge-tier.yaml, module.yaml._

### Session Setup

_AI-recommended three-phase approach: First Principles Thinking (strip assumptions) → Morphological Analysis (map design space) → Chaos Engineering (stress test). User confirmed approach._

## Technique Selection

**Approach:** AI-Recommended Techniques
**Analysis Context:** System-level infrastructure design with cross-cutting concerns across installer, source resolution, CCC indexing, state management, and multi-project sharing

**Recommended Techniques:**

- **First Principles Thinking:** Strip away assumptions baked into the ephemeral model, rebuild from fundamental truths about what SKF needs from source access. Discover genuine use cases beyond clone caching.
- **Morphological Analysis:** Systematically map every design parameter (git strategy, staleness, layout, cleanup, CCC lifecycle, sharing) and explore combinations to prevent locking into the first obvious design.
- **Chaos Engineering:** Deliberately break the design — disk full, concurrent forges, upstream deletion, corrupted index — to catalog failure modes before party mode.

**AI Rationale:** Infrastructure design benefits from stripping assumptions first (avoid carrying ephemeral-model baggage), then systematic parameter exploration (the design space is large), then destruction testing (the user's explicit goal is finding breaking changes). This sequence produces a stress-tested design ready for multi-agent party mode review.

input.new_string:
**AI Rationale:** Infrastructure design benefits from stripping assumptions first (avoid carrying ephemeral-model baggage), then systematic parameter exploration (the design space is large), then destruction testing (the user's explicit goal is finding breaking changes). This sequence produces a stress-tested design ready for multi-agent party mode review.

## Phase 1: First Principles Thinking — Results

### Fundamental Truths Established
1. SKF's relationship with source repos is **read-only** — never writes to source
2. SKF needs a **specific ref** (branch/tag/commit), not just "latest"
3. CCC needs a **local file tree** to index — can't index remote URLs
4. The **same repo may be forged from multiple projects** — nothing project-specific about the source
5. **Index quality improves with full context** — shallow clones miss some content

### Assumptions Killed
- "Clone is temporary" → The workspace is persistent
- "Indexes are disposable" → The CCC index is the most valuable artifact
- "Repos are per-session" → Machine-level shared resource
- "Clone is the action, indexing is a side-effect" → Index-first architecture
- "The workspace is a file cache" → It's a knowledge substrate

### Key Architectural Decisions
- **Layered design**: Layer 0 (persistent clones + CCC) → Layer 1 (registry) → Layer 2 (optional tool tenants like graphify) → Layer 3 (cross-repo intelligence)
- **Tool-agnostic persistence**: Tools persist outputs inside repo checkouts naturally
- **Graphify as future tenant, not foundation**: 16.6k stars but pre-alpha stability (v0.3.24, security bugs still being found weekly)
- **Zero-glue CCC integration**: CCC daemon is already system-level with per-request connections and project registry. Workspace repos just need a path change.
- **Cross-platform requirement**: Linux/macOS/Windows support mandatory

### CCC Daemon Architecture Insight
CCC runs as an invisible daemon (auto-start on first use, auto-upgrade on version mismatch, per-request Unix socket connections). The workspace gets persistent indexing for free just by preserving the checkout directory. `ccc search --refresh` handles re-indexing after git fetch.

### Ideas Generated (#1-#18)
| # | Title | Category |
|---|-------|----------|
| 1 | Read-Only Source Cache | Foundational |
| 2 | Index-First Architecture | Foundational |
| 3 | Machine-Level Shared Resource | Foundational |
| 4 | Workspace Is a Knowledge Graph, Not File Cache | Foundational |
| 5 | Multi-Modal Extraction Changes "Source" Meaning | Foundational |
| 6 | Cross-Repo Knowledge Graph Enables Stack Skills | Foundational |
| 7 | Layered Tool Synergy, Not Replacement | Foundational |
| 8 | Tool-Agnostic Workspace | Architecture |
| 9 | Registry as Cross-Project Memory | Architecture |
| 10 | Graphify as Future Tenant, Not Foundation | Architecture |
| 11 | Shallow-to-Deep Clone Upgrade Path | Architecture |
| 12 | Graphify Accommodation Pattern | Research |
| 13 | Tool Maturity Gate for Layer 2 Tenants | Research |
| 14 | Registry Future-Proofing for Graph Data | Research |
| 15 | GitNexus as Alternative Watch | Research |
| 16 | Zero-Glue CCC Integration | Architecture |
| 17 | Daemon-Aware Staleness | Architecture |
| 18 | Shared Daemon, Multiplied Value | Architecture |

content.text:
Now Phase 2. Let me load the technique execution reference for Morphological Analysis.

---

## Phase 2: Morphological Analysis

We're going to map every design parameter, list all viable options for each, then systematically evaluate combinations. This is where the architecture becomes concrete.

**Cross-platform constraint noted** — every parameter choice must work on Linux, macOS, and Windows. This eliminates Unix-only assumptions (symlinks, Unix sockets in paths, case-sensitive filesystems, etc.).

Here are the **7 key parameters** I've identified. Let's work through them one at a time:

---

### Parameter 1: Workspace Location & Naming

| Option | Path | Pros | Cons | Cross-Platform |
|--------|------|------|------|----------------|
| **A** | `~/.skf-workspace/` | Clear branding, hidden by convention | Dot-prefix hidden on Linux/macOS but visible in Windows Explorer | Partial — Windows doesn't hide dot-dirs by default |
| **B** | `~/.local/share/skf/workspace/` | XDG-compliant on Linux | Doesn't exist on macOS/Windows without setup | Linux-biased |
| **C** | Platform-specific via `appdirs` | `~/.local/share/skf/` (Linux), `~/Library/Application Support/skf/` (macOS), `%LOCALAPPDATA%\skf\` (Windows) | Different paths per OS, harder to document | Best practice for cross-platform |
| **D** | `~/.skf/workspace/` | Short, groups all SKF state under `~/.skf/` | Competing with potential future SKF config | Good |
| **E** | Configurable with smart default | Default to `~/.skf/workspace/`, allow override via `SKF_WORKSPACE` env var or `config.yaml` | More complexity | Best flexibility |

**My recommendation: Option E (configurable) with Option D as default.**

Rationale:
- `~/.skf/` as a root groups config + workspace + any future SKF global state
- `SKF_WORKSPACE` env var lets CI, Docker, and corporate setups override
- On Windows, `~` resolves to `%USERPROFILE%` (e.g., `C:\Users\armel\.skf\workspace\`)
- Dot-prefix is conventional for tool state directories on all platforms

**What's your take? Or should I move through all 7 parameters first and we discuss at the end?**
